retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Yellow zone: The yellow zone encompasses peak flow values between 50% and 80% of the patient's personal best. Patients employ their home action plan, beginning with bronchodilators, when they identify readings in this zone. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate candidates for peak flow rate measurement based on clinical indications, such as suspected asthma, severity monitoring, or treatment response assessment. Screen patients for proper technique and provide tailored education on the correct use of peak flow meters to optimize data accuracy. Apply evidence-based guidelines to interpret peak flow data and make informed decisions regarding asthma management. Implement strategies to collaborate with other healthcare team members to integrate peak flow measurement into comprehensive asthma management protocols. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician should observe the patient's technique during in-office education and assist with necessary adjustments. Clinicians should also review the technique at subsequent follow-up visits. Peak flow rates in individual patients tend to decrease over time, likely from waning skill and effort. [11] This observation highlights the need for ongoing retraining of techniques after the initial clinic visit. Some authors recommend retraining at every follow-up visit in conjunction with inhaler retraining. [12] Clinicians should reestablish patients' personal best annually to assess for growth in children and disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Typical peak flow meters come equipped with gauge markers indicating 3 "zones" that can be set by the patient or clinician to help users interpret their peak flow values. For ease of patient interpretation, the colors of a traditional traffic light designate varying degrees of peak flow limitation. The designated zones correlate with a written asthma action plan. Green zone: This zone designates peak flow values of 80% to 100% of the patient's personal best. In the absence of symptoms, the patient continues with their current regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: PEF measurement is also integral to managing acute asthma exacerbations, especially in adolescents and adults. While used less frequently during pediatric exacerbations, the technique remains valuable in routine asthma care. Clinicians must ensure patients understand how to use the device correctly and interpret the results to optimize outcomes. Adhering to standardized guidelines for PEF meters and ensuring devices meet established criteria further enhances the reliability of measurements in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should be cautioned against over-reliance on peak flow measurements alone. Studies evaluating the overall outcomes when utilizing peak flow measurement alone show conflicting results and reveal no advantage of using PEF monitoring over symptom monitoring to guide self-management. [14] Society guidelines have suggested a comprehensive approach that accounts for both subjective symptoms and objective data from the peak flow meter. Data show that this approach can decrease healthcare utilization.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Evaluation -- Peak Flow Meter
retrieved_docs.content: Patients repeat this process 3 times, recording the highest reading as the current peak flow measurement. Patients can compare their recorded values to established graphs based on age and height for adults and height for adolescents to determine their predicted value. Notably, reduced peak flow values are not specific to asthma. Patients with either an obstructive or restrictive pattern on spirometry can have decreased peak flow values. Additionally, the accuracy of results is highly contingent on patient effort.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance -- Pediatrics
retrieved_docs.content: Children aged 5 or older can use the peak flow meter with reliable results. However, cooperation with the technique may limit the accuracy during acute exacerbations, and clinicians should interpret the results cautiously. For this reason, healthcare professionals do not routinely use peak flow measurements during acute asthma exacerbations in children. Tables of normal peak flow rates are available for children and adolescents. Additionally, when children use a peak flow monitor for self-management and assessment, clinicians should consider providing a second meter for the child to keep at school in the nurse's office should concerns arise.

retrieved_docs.content: AIAN youth and adults have the highest cigarette smoking rate among all racial and ethnic groups in the United States. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance -- Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: Despite body changes associated with pregnancy, peak flow rate and personal best numbers do not change through each trimester or during the postpartum period. Changes in the observed PEF in pregnant women should be treated as worsening obstruction until proven otherwise. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance -- Limitations
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned previously, suboptimal effort can lead to falsely decreased readings. Chest wall disease, obesity, and muscle weakness can also reduce PEF without airflow limitation. In patients with severe asthma, peak flow measurement can underestimate the degree of airflow limitation. [14] Patients often have difficulty adhering to long-term peak flow monitoring. [15] Routine measurement is most effective when limited to shorter periods.

retrieved_docs.title: Peak Flow Rate Measurement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Red zone: Patients in the red zone have peak flow measurements below 50% of their personal best. They should begin bronchodilator therapy immediately and contact their clinician if their peak flow measurements do not immediately return to the yellow or green zones. Peak flow measurements in this range indicate that severe airway obstruction may be present and is a medical emergency. See StatPearls' companion references " Asthma " and " Pediatric Asthma " for a detailed description of the management of acute asthma exacerbations in adults and children.
